quoteFirst: Since high water at Dover on the 26th of August occurred at 6.21 a.m., and since the tidal stream, according to one of Calver’s observations, turned 3 hours, 40 minutes after high water, the stream may have turned westward at 10.1 a.m. Assuming that the duration of the westward stream was 5 hours, 53 minutes--the same as that recorded on one occasion by Calver--the stream would have ceased at
quoteLast: But Calver’s observations were made ‘in comparatively still weather’;[2997] and, to quote Admiral Beechey, ‘winds greatly affect the time of turn of the stream.’
title: Ancient Britain and the Invasions of Julius Caesar
author: Holmes, T. Rice (Thomas Rice), 1855-1933

quoteFirst: It is clear, therefore, that on the day of Caesar’s landing the stream may have turned eastward earlier than
quoteLast: ; and if it turned twenty-five minutes earlier, it turned in ‘the ninth hour’.
title: Ancient Britain and the Invasions of Julius Caesar
author: Holmes, T. Rice (Thomas Rice), 1855-1933

quoteFirst: The ninth hour in the latitude of Dover lasted on the 26th of August from 2.20 till 3.30 p.m.[3234] Therefore Caesar, assuming that his statement was literally correct, may have waited at anchor for the arrival of the laggard ships until 3.30 p.m. Now, as the reader will remember, I have demonstrated, from the evidence to which Airy appeals--the evidence supplied by Admiral Beechey and Surveyor Calver--that on the day of Caesar’s landing the tidal stream may have turned eastward earlier than
quoteLast: ;[3235] and if it turned twenty-five minutes earlier, it turned in the ninth hour.
title: Ancient Britain and the Invasions of Julius Caesar
author: Holmes, T. Rice (Thomas Rice), 1855-1933
